intro:
Coat of mail in the King James Version describes the body armor worn by warriors in the ancient Near East, consisting of overlapping metal scales attached to a leather or fabric backing — giving the appearance and flexible protection of fish scales. The Hebrew term is shiryon qasqasim, literally "armor of scales." Goliath wore a coat of mail weighing five thousand shekels of bronze (approximately 125 pounds), described in 1 Samuel 17:5 as part of his formidable panoply that included a bronze helmet, bronze greaves, and a bronze javelin.

Scale armor was common across the ancient Near East — examples have been recovered from Egyptian, Canaanite, and Mesopotamian contexts — and was the standard heavy protection for elite infantry and chariot warriors. King Ahab was also wearing armor in his chariot when he was shot by a random arrow at Ramoth-gilead that found the joint between his breastplate and scale armor and proved fatal (1 Kings 22:34). The vulnerability at the joints of overlapping scale armor is a recurring feature of ancient military accounts.
